# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Is there a movie where villain actually won ?

**Score:** 283
**Body:** I’m looking for movie recommendations where the antagonist actually succeeds by the end — not a fake win, not a last-minute moral reversal, and not a “technically the villain but actually misunderstood” situation.

I want stories where:

The villain’s plan works

The ending feels unsettling, bleak, or ironic

The film commits to the outcome instead of playing safe

Any genre is fine — thriller, sci-fi, crime, psychological, even horror — as long as the ending doesn’t pull its punch.

Please avoid spoilers if possible.😟

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Watchmen?
2. Seven…
3. Chinatown (1974)
4. Fallen, the villan wins by surviving.
5. The Social Network
6. Se7en, No Country for Old Men, The Usual Suspects, Oldboy, Primal Fear
  - Arlington Road!
7. Nightcrawler
8. Gone Girl
9. Brazil

## 2. If you haven’t seen Prisoners (2013) yet, I highly recommend it

**Score:** 75
**Body:** Watched Prisoners recently and I’m honestly surprised I put it off for so long.  
The tension, performances (Hugh Jackman & Jake Gyllenhaal), and atmosphere are top-tier from start to finish.

It’s dark, slow-burn, and really sticks with you after it ends.  
If you like psychological thrillers that don’t hold your hand, this is a must-watch.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. If you liked *Prisoners*, you might also enjoy *Zodiac* or *Gone Girl* similar slow-burn tension.
  - Denis Villenueve and David Fincher do share some filmmaking traits so this tracks
2. thank you!
3. how is this not a barred suggestion yet, mods?
4. Prisoners would stand a good chance at being a good movie if it didn’t have a big, giant flaw in it … and the flaw is Paul. Dano is weak sauce, man. He’s a weak sister.
  - I see what you did there 🤣
5. It has tension, mystery, grey shades of morality, and  respect for the audiences' intelligence. All the best aspects of a great thriller.

Add to that a great cast and great pacing. One of the best this century so far I'd say..
6. Big big favourite of mine, it’s so good.
7. I watched Prisoners for the first time (on DVD) last week and it's one of the best movies I have seen this year. It reminded me of Weapons a little, but it is a much better movie. It's got great cinematography by Roger Deakins too. I highly recommend it if you like tense crime thrillers.
8. If you love prisoners, i recommend brothers (2004)

**Score:** 36
**Body:** I would love it even more if the movies are just dark, gothic, ritualistic, type stuff LOLLLL but anything that will just mess with my head and perspective. Thank you so much!!!

## 4. Just watched Four Lions and In the Loop — craving more sharp, dark, quintessentially British satire. Any recs?

**Score:** 31
**Body:** Hey everyone,

I just watched **Four Lions** and **In the Loop** back to back and absolutely loved both of them. The dry, brutal dialogue, the political / social satire, and that very specific *British* kind of dark humor really clicked for me.

I’m looking for movie recommendations along those lines — smart, cynical, uncomfortable in a funny way, and very much *quintessentially British*. Not really looking for flashy action or stylized stuff (so not things like Kingsman), more like sharp writing, bleak comedy, and satire that punches up.

Films, not TV (though if there’s an absolute must-watch series I won’t complain).

Any suggestions?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Death of Stalin
2. The death of stalin

But if you really enjoyed - in the loop - check out the tv series -the thick of it. In the loop was a spin off from the UK series. 

Also check out -brass eye -and -day today.
3. Please please please watch the series Inside Number 9.
4. Brass Eye
5. Alan Partridge Series
6. Anything by Chris Morris or Armando Iannucci
7. 'Monkey Dust'

Sorry, it's a series. But if it's bleak, dark comedy that you're after, then they don't get much bleaker, or darker.

It's great :-)
8. Just watched Alma’s Not Normal. It’s fairly damning of the care system and a sharp comedic take on intergenerational trauma.

Also: The Day Today and Brass Eye. Never have there been better news/current affairs satires.
9. The thick of it, in the loop is the movie spin off
10. The Guard (2011)

## 5. Looking for movies with a Satan archetype

**Score:** 24
**Body:** Movies that comes to mind are Constantine, The Devils Advocate, Passion of the Christ. Something where there’s a Satan/Devil presence preferably more drama leaning but not against sci fi. Not exactly looking for horror where Satan is just there to scare. I want them to be a part of the influence and plot of the movie in dramatic fashion

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Angel Heart
2. Bedazzled
3. The Ninth Gate. More of a literary take on the Devil
4. The Prophecy (1995)
5. Little Nicky
6. The Stand is a mini series, but Randall Flagg fits the profile
7. Legend (1985)
8. Time Bandits(1981)
9. Tenacious D and the Pick of Destiny
10. End Of Days

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. 2025 movies that exceeded or didn’t live up to your expectations

**Score:** 303
**Body:** What’s a movie that came out this year that you thought would be ok but was actually awesome? What’s a movie that you were excited to see but didn’t and up being great?

For me, F1 seemed like it would be a solid racing movie but was actually an adrenaline rush I loved. I was really looking forward to new Bong Joon Ho but I was disappointed with Mickey 17. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Mickey 17 was THIS year? Good god this year was long.
2. loved the worldbuilding in mickey 17, the movie went downhill from the third act
  - I’m so mad because it could’ve been so good. The ethics of printing, the illegal duplicates, the relationship dynamics, all fantastic stuff and then they go away from all of it to focus on the alien baby smh. This is going in our episode for sure.
    - For a better take on it, go with "Moon" starring Sam Rockwell and Sam Rockwell.
  - It couldn’t decide which movie it was. So it was all the movies. Like they did a brainstorming session and kept everything.
3. A film that I thought was just okay, but turned out to be amazing - that's Steven Soderbergh's Black Bag.

A film I was eagerly awaiting but never got around to watching (I kept putting it off because of other things), and then it turned out to be brilliant - Ryan Coogler's Sinners.

My disappointment of the year is Mission: Impossible - The Final Reckoning.
  - > My disappointment of the year is Mission: Impossible - The Final Reckoning

Same. Could've been better with a 2 hour runtime.
4. Mickey 17 didn’t live up to expectations. F1 was badass.
5. F1 is one of those movies you had to see in IMAX
6. Mickey 17 was bad. So much potential wasted and such a weird execution.

It’s a Joker-themed psychological thriller and Scorsese tribute about a victim of brain damage who is failed by a culture that throws mentally ill and disabled people under the bus. He ends up in an asylum, not much room for a sequel, he has no potential as a supervillain.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Sinners had a far more interesting first half, and it’s least interesting aspect was the action and vampires as action fodder. Hot take?

**Score:** 445
**Body:** The acclaim that Sinners has been receiving as an all rounder of a film has been gnawing at me since its release. I had always found the social and cultural commentary, around music, racial dynamics that affect individual experiences and the folklore aspect that retains itself regardless of the advancement of time so fkn fascinating. The contrast between the performance of the songs “I Lied to You” and “Rocky Road to Dublin” were so damn fascinating. But the movie lost me a wee bit as it became an all out action romp. 

Maybe I’m simplifying it too much for myself, but the moment all these threads coalesced into the action set piece, I thought it lost some of its grandeur almost? The vampires felt slightly less complex a threat (which sounds strange considering the outcomes of the conflict). I dunno, I loved the quiet violence and character work that the film offered across the first half and the leg end of the film, but the action itself, for me, diluted some of the complexity that the film offered. 

Would love to hear any and all opinions around this. Cheers!

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I kind of agree. The setup was full of great character work and commentary, but all of that sort of gets forgotten once the actions starts. I really wish the thematics and character arcs were more fully integrated into the action pay off. The end result is that due to a lack of follow through a lot of the great setup becomes texture rather than a meaningful part of the overall text.
  - This was definitely the issue. The character work never came through as well within the action aspects. It felt like the character work was setting up for audience empathy when characters were offed
2. Not a hot take. Ive seen plenty of takes that share a similar opinion as yours. It did feel different in the latter half, but I didnt mind. Maybe theres a better way to integrate vampires into a period drama like this, but I didnt mind how this one played out.
3. I think it's most a problem of the action/vampire section getting very sloppy in the filmmaking. Its just overall less we executed form the garlic test scene onwards..

Coogler decided to riff on The Thing's blood test scene bust ist just completely unable to build that level of tension. One major problem is that for whatever reason, they didn't really settle on how many people are in the scene! *Any of these people could be a vampire Wait, who is that guy in the wide shot? Wait. Where did he go?* Similar problem when the vampires actually attack . We need some more people to get attacked so suddenly there are even more randos.
  - Yeah, the tension in that scene was just poorly written. I expected the girl to refuse the garlic because she’s allergic and it could kill her (as a human) but instead she just didn’t like it? and that was a good enough reason to not do the vampire test? didn’t really match the tone of the rest of the scene or film
4. I loved the first half and found the second half a little frustrating and tonally muddled, especially how it treated the deaths of half of the characters it took the time to humanize in the first half as total jokes.
5. This was my main takeaway after watching the film for the first time recently. I thought Coogler had a really good film in there with what was going on in the first half. The From Dusk Till Dawn reveal is what ruins the movie for me, and the final act shootout was really not a good idea. Neither was the way that the vampires were ultimately defeated. And honestly, even the fight scenes in the joint once they finally got in were really bad and not very smart.

I'm sorry, but there's no way that those few humans stood a chance against all of those vampires indoors. There's also no way the vampires are just standing there and letting the sun explode them. I was so into the movie until all of this stuff started happening.

I don't know how he thought it was a good idea for us to go from the past era Mo' Better Blues to a lesser version of From Dusk Till Dawn. I really wish the movie would have remained about what we got in the first half. Every scene and every character was so rich up until the action started. Even the dialogue that was so strong in the beginning didn't hold up then.


In the end it felt like two completely different movies, and I really wish that I could see what happened with the first movie

Edit: I also think if you're going to lean into the vampire thing, after setting up the amazing vision of the blues being sacred, and getting taken away, just like the freedom of the slaves, then you should have put a little more of a clever spin on it.

At first I expected something like that. I thought maybe he was going to go the way of realizing the only way they could get freedom is to become vampires, and then take the revenge against the klan as vampires, instead of that shootout. I'm not a writer but that's just the thought I had, and I thought it would have been a better end.
  - > Edit: I also think if you're going to lean into the vampire thing, after setting up the amazing vision of the blues being sacred, and getting taken away, just like the freedom of the slaves, then you should have put a little more of a clever spin on it.
> 
> 

This was definitely one of my main takeaways. Like others, I enjoyed the film overall, and the first half was very strong. 

I was quite surprised at, ultimately, how typical the treatment of the vampire mythology was. I was expecting some fresh twist, but it was a case of a) they won't come in unless invited b) they were suspectible to garlic, silver and the usual methods c) they couldn't be around in the day time,.

I think they should have fully leant into the music angle, and had that be something different with the vampires.
6. Agreed. Loved the setup and was mostly annoyed by the end. And then the mid credit scene sort of left a bad taste in my mouth. The choice to place it there felt a little pretentious after the formulaic vampire action that had just ensued. It was still enjoyable though, but closer to a 7/10 for me.
7. I did like the vampires and I think those characters could have been tied into the themes introduced in the first half of the film. It’s the action itself that I found very rushed and unsatisfying. The garlic scene and all of the scenes that happen at the door were really tense and exciting, and I wish He’d leaned a little more into that kind of feel to give us a more suspenseful, less action-packed third act. I think that could’ve left more room for the ideas and characters that are so great about the first half.

## 2. Lynne Ramsay and the subjective camera. Deconstructing the "Portrait" in Die My Love

**Score:** 11
**Body:** In *Die My Love*, Lynne Ramsay moves away from the narrative fragmentation of *You Were Never Really Here* toward a more static, voyeuristic visual language. The choice to shoot in the 1.37:1 Academy ratio serves two functions: it emphasizes the "portrait" nature of Grace’s character study and physically traps the characters within the frame.

The opening shot - a long, locked-off wide down a hallway sets a tone of cold observation that denies the audience the easy empathy of a close-up. 

Seamus McGarvey’s cinematography utilizes day-for-night techniques that lend the exterior scenes an eerie, liminal quality, suggesting that for Grace, time has ceased to be linear. By the time we reach the wedding sequence, the film has entirely abandoned objective reality for a fever-dream stream of consciousness. 

It is a rigorous piece of filmmaking that rejects the trauma-dump cliches of modern prestige drama in favor of something far more primal and unexplainable.

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 3. Help me understand Trenque Lauqen

**Score:** 1
**Body:** Four and a half hours later and I finish Argentine film Trenque Lauqen. I have a lot of disparate thoughts going through my mind and still processing what I have just experienced. On the one hand, the film felt somewhat poetic with the first half of the film feeling very different from the second half in terms of genre while maintaining a quirky tone.
There is also a cyclical nature to the film: two love triangles, two women wandering off into the wilderness etc.
I realise the film is pointing toward the them of how it may be better to keep some mysteries unresolved, yet I am interested to hear how other people interpreted the film and its ending.
For me, I got the sense that Laura's journey ended up mirroring that of Carmen's in the first half. Almost as though pursuing the very myserty itself became something that would consume Laura until she herself would become a myserty to the World around her.

I am not sure I understood all the links between the creature in the lake, the girl who was the apparition and her partner, the request that they made to Laura, or even what the final scene meant. It's likely that my brain was fried during the final 30 minutes or so. Can anyone help piece this beautiful and poetic puzzle together? Thanks

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 4. Platoon & Boyz n the Hood: Films where the director makes themselves the main character

**Score:** 2
**Body:** I’m not sure if there’s an academic term for this, but I call it “Stephen King Syndrome” based on how Jack Torrance is depicted in the Shining novel compared to the film version. 

I should be clear, the main characters in Platoon and Boyz n the Hood are effective because the audience can relate to them, but since both films are social commentaries, in both intentions and interpretations, the message and story become weaker in a deeper analysis. 

This is more noticeable on a rewatch, and especially in comparison to alternative (I’ll say superior) films in their genres, Full Metal Jacket and Menace II Society. 

In Platoon and Boyz n the Hood, the main character is already morally good and is trying to remain good in an environment that can easily corrupt them, but it feels that plot points only happen for their character development. Other side characters serve the main character, not the wider social commentary. Even if Oliver Stones and John Singleton real life experiences helped shaped their films, it’s pretty noticeable that they essentially inserted their own morals and personality onto the main character. It honestly limits the story.

Kubrick and the Hughes Brothers make their characters more equal in status and situation, so it feels genuine when the barrel of the gun isn’t prejudiced against any particular character. Soldiers all have the same jobs, to follow orders and to kill. While gangsters are gangsters to cops, civilians, and other gangsters, social perception matters more than how someone can be like. 

I seen many others say Platoon and Boyz n the Hood feel dated in their respective genres, but I think it’s because of this reason. What do you think ? 

Regardless, I still respect the stories told by Stone and Singleton. 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I'm not sure this is always a bad thing.

I mean, Marcello Mastroianni in 8½ is basically Federico Fellini. Roy Scheider in All That Jazz is Bob Fosse. The autobiographical aspect is a big part of what makes both films so interesting. 

Like anything in a movie, I guess it's whether it's done well or not.
2. I don’t think either movie feels dated, especially Platoon. Yeah Stone may have inserted himself as the main character but also….what an interesting character? Someone who thinks he’s fighting for an ideal but ends up learning that things are very much the opposite. The movie also touches on the black struggle in Vietnam, a story that hasn’t fully been told. 

I personally like Apocalypse Now much better. However something I feel is lacking in Apocalypse Now is any real character development; Willard is just as jaded and crazy as he starts out, Kurtz doesn’t change and is also just as crazy. They are men driven mad, but we don’t really see that. Platoon you see an idealistic young man become wrecked by the horrors of war. He comes out in the end okay but he’s changed forever by the experience. 

Charlie Sheen does a fantastic job (he really could’ve been a fantastic actor had he not fried his brain), the cinematography, music and production values don’t feel dated at all. Tom Berenger is amazing. The only part about the movie that feels off is the “Old Country Buffet” style font used in the opening credits.
  - You should consider starting a thread about Oliver Stone. 

He has a mixed, controversial legacy in 2025 and it might be interesting to discuss it as a whole.
3. I will fight you on behalf of both of these movies. Platoon is my literal favorite film of all time and Boyz n the Hood is also extremely good -- i've watched it a lot. I love these types of movies.

They are dated in that they're conventional within their era, but they're still amazing films. I also think you have a certain perspective when you know that the main character can't die. And that's not good or bad -- it's just one constraint on the storytelling.

Also, you still have the matter of how reliable the narrator is. I'll call out Badlands, where Sissy Spacek is a kind of dull-witted teenage girl. Yes, she's the main character and you want to like her -- but she's enabling Starkweather, so she's not at all reliable and arguably she's a force for evil as well. Similarly, the main character in BitH is trying his best, but he's in a tough situation where he's not always making the most moral decisions, and Sheen is awesome in Platoon because he's a superior dickbag who thinks he knows how things work, and he's so wrong. And people get harmed. I actually think he's not Stone at all -- he's a criticism of ostensibly well-meaning liberals who attempted to influence the conflict without actually understanding it or being willing to engage in it, logistically or even intellectually. So I don't think that convention is limiting -- in the hands of a self-aware filmmaker.
  - >I actually think he's not Stone at all -- he's a criticism of ostensibly well-meaning liberals who attempted to influence the conflict without actually understanding it or being willing to engage in it, logistically or even intellectually. 

Some would say that that absolutely describes pre-2010s Oliver Stone.
    - You do realize that Stone fought in the Vietnam War, right? It's kinda silly to say that he wasn't willing to engage in the conflict when he literally joined the infantry and fought.
      - I meant that that criticism could apply to Stone as a filmmaker/political activist/public figure.
4. It's fun to play around with film theory like this, but I would work on elaborating where you say that it "limits the story".   

If anything, it leads to great Hollywood movies where the main character doesn't have as much of an impact on audiences. Because in both cases, the films were highly recognized with awards groups but the lead actors were not.
5. I think that you’re conflating a few different ideas based on your personal preferences and what you know about the movies. There are a million great stories about a morally good character being thrust into a potentially corrupting situation (The Great Gatsby is a classic example) There are also works where, if you know something about the author, you can tell that the protagonist is a thinly veiled proxy for themselves (King does this in The Shining and Misery). But the two tropes aren’t synonymous (Platoon might be vaguely autobiographical, but Wall Street isn’t).

## 5. Comparison: Hollywood Movies vs Prestige TV

**Score:** 0
**Body:** With Netflix buying WB and people generally preferring to stream at home over going to the cinema, the line between film and television keeps getting blurred. So I thought of comparing the best of prestige TV and Hollywood movies, by looking at each medium's most defining examples, how the likes of The Godfather (1 & 2), 2001, Taxi Driver, Apocalypse Now, Vertigo, Citizen Kane, There Will Be Blood etc. competes against the likes of Breaking Bad, The Sopranos, The Wire, Better Call Saul, Mr. Robot etc. Given the differences in the format, the movies and the TV shows excel at different qualities. The TV Shows thrive at character development and detailed stories due to their serialized nature and length, giving them a novelistic quality. While the Movies have a much more painterly quality to them due to their tight focus, thriving in the craft of filmmaking and visual storytelling. That isn't to say that the movies from the examples I mentioned have no novelistic quality to them, or the TV Shows don't have any painterly quality to them, but one is just better than the other in each quality. So I guess it comes down to what quality you prefer, or what quality is better suited. Personally, I have always been drawn to the pure filmmaking qualities of the movies, which makes them a unique art form, giving them a type of richness that I don't find in the mentioned TV Shows. But then again I think generally people prefer extensive stories over the artistic qualities of filmmaking. What do you people think??

(BTW, this post isn't about the feature format vs serialized format. When made by the same artist, the two format can thrive at similar qualities, like Mulholland Dr. and Twin Peaks. But it's not the norm which is what this post is about, taking a look at the best examples that define each medium and compare them)
